first_paragraph: [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") is a country in [Northern Europe](/wiki/Northern_Europe "Northern Europe") on the [Scandinavian](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") Peninsula. It borders [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") to the west (which is one of Sweden’s non-[EU](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") neighbours); [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") to the northeast; and the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea") and [Gulf of Bothnia](/wiki/Gulf_of_Bothnia "Gulf of Bothnia") to the south and east. At 450,295 km2 (173,860 sq mi), Sweden is the largest country in Northern Europe, the fifth largest in Europe, and the 55th largest country in the world.

[Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") is a country in [Northern Europe](/wiki/Northern_Europe "Northern Europe") on the [Scandinavian](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") Peninsula. It borders [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") to the west (which is one of Sweden’s non-[EU](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") neighbours); [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") to the northeast; and the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea") and [Gulf of Bothnia](/wiki/Gulf_of_Bothnia "Gulf of Bothnia") to the south and east. At 450,295 km2 (173,860 sq mi), Sweden is the largest country in Northern Europe, the fifth largest in Europe, and the 55th largest country in the world.

Sweden has a 3,572 km (2,220 mi) long coastline on its east, and the [Scandinavian mountain chain](/wiki/Scandinavian_mountain_chain "Scandinavian mountain chain") (Skanderna) on its western border, separating it from Norway. It has maritime borders with [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") (another non-[EU](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") neighbour) [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania"), [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia") and [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia"), and it is also linked to [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") (southwest) by the [Öresund bridge](/wiki/%C3%98resund_Bridge "Øresund Bridge"). It has an [Exclusive Economic Zone](/wiki/Exclusive_Economic_Zone "Exclusive Economic Zone") of 160,885 km2 (62,118 mi2).

## Terrain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terrain")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Map_of_Sweden_Cities_%28polar_stereographic%29.png/250px-Map_of_Sweden_Cities_%28polar_stereographic%29.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Sweden_Cities_(polar_stereographic).png)

Map of Sweden

Much of Sweden is heavily forested, with 69%[[1]](#cite_note-1) of the country being forest and woodland, while farmland constitutes only 8% of land use.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Sweden consists of 39,960 km2
of water area, constituting around 95,700 lakes.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[A]](#cite_note-5) The lakes are sometimes used for [water power](/wiki/Water_power "Water power") plants, especially the large northern rivers and lakes.

Most of northern and western central Sweden consists of vast tracts of hilly and mountainous land called the [Norrland terrain](/wiki/Norrland_terrain "Norrland terrain").[[5]](#cite_note-6) From the south the transition to the Norrland terrain is not only seen in the relief but also in the wide and contiguous [boreal forests](/wiki/Taiga "Taiga") that extend north of it[[6]](#cite_note-7) with [till](/wiki/Till "Till") and [peat](/wiki/Peat "Peat") being the overwhelmingly most common [soil types](/wiki/Soil_type "Soil type").[[7]](#cite_note-Lundq1969-8)

South of the Norrland terrain lies the [Central Swedish lowland](/wiki/Central_Swedish_lowland "Central Swedish lowland") which forms a broad east-west trending belt from [Gothenburg](/wiki/Gothenburg "Gothenburg") to [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm").[[8]](#cite_note-Naten-9)[[9]](#cite_note-Worldregional-10) This is the traditional heartland of Sweden due to its large population and [agricultural resources](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Sweden "Agriculture in Sweden").[[9]](#cite_note-Worldregional-10) The region forms a belt of fertile soils suitable for agriculture that interrupts the [forested](/wiki/Scandinavian_and_Russian_taiga "Scandinavian and Russian taiga") and [till-coated](/wiki/Till "Till") lands to the north and south.[[10]](#cite_note-Ytbildning-11) Before the expansion of agriculture, these fertile soils were covered by a [broad-leaved tree](/wiki/Broad-leaved_tree "Broad-leaved tree") forest where [maples](/wiki/Acer_platanoides "Acer platanoides"), [oaks](/wiki/Oak "Oak"), [ashes](/wiki/Fraxinus_excelsior "Fraxinus excelsior"), [small-leaved lime](/wiki/Tilia_cordata "Tilia cordata") and [common hazel](/wiki/Corylus_avellana "Corylus avellana") grew. The Central Swedish lowland does however also contain soils of poor quality, particularly in hills where [Scots pine](/wiki/Scots_pine "Scots pine") and [Norway spruce](/wiki/Picea_abies "Picea abies") grow on top of thin till soils.[[10]](#cite_note-Ytbildning-11) Agriculture aside, the region benefits also from the proximity of [hydropower](/wiki/Hydropower "Hydropower"), forest and [bergslagen](/wiki/Bergslagen "Bergslagen")'s mineral resources.[[9]](#cite_note-Worldregional-10) Sweden's four largest lakes, [Vänern](/wiki/V%C3%A4nern "Vänern"), [Vättern](/wiki/V%C3%A4ttern "Vättern"), [Mälaren](/wiki/Lake_M%C3%A4laren "Lake Mälaren") and [Hjälmaren](/wiki/Hj%C3%A4lmaren "Hjälmaren"), lie within the lowlands.[[10]](#cite_note-Ytbildning-11)

To the south of the Central Swedish lowland lies the [South Swedish highlands](/wiki/South_Swedish_highlands "South Swedish highlands")[[8]](#cite_note-Naten-9) which except for a lack of deep valleys is similar to the [Norrland terrain](/wiki/Norrland_terrain "Norrland terrain") found further north in Sweden.[[7]](#cite_note-Lundq1969-8) The highest point of the highlands lies at 377 m.[[11]](#cite_note-LB2013-12) Poor soil conditions have posed significant difficulties for agriculture in the highlands, meaning that over time small industries became relatively important in local economies.[[12]](#cite_note-skogskunskap-13)

Southernmost Sweden contains a varied landscape with both plains and hilly terrain. A characteristic chain of elongated hills runs across Scania from northwest to southeast. These hills are [horsts](/wiki/Horst_(geology) "Horst (geology)") located along the [Tornquist Zone](/wiki/Trans-European_Suture_Zone "Trans-European Suture Zone").[[13]](#cite_note-NatenSkaneterrang-14)[[14]](#cite_note-NatenSkaneberggrund-15) Some of the horsts are [Hallandsåsen](/wiki/Hallands%C3%A5sen "Hallandsåsen"), [Römelåsen](/w/index.php?title=R%C3%B6mel%C3%A5sen&action=edit&redlink=1 "Römelåsen (page does not exist)") and [Söderåsen](/wiki/S%C3%B6der%C3%A5sen "Söderåsen").[[13]](#cite_note-NatenSkaneterrang-14) The plains of Scania and [Halland](/wiki/Halland "Halland") make up 10% of Sweden's cultivated lands and are the country's main agricultural landscape. Productivity is high relative to the rest of Sweden and more akin to that of more southern European countries.[[15]](#cite_note-NatenProduktion-16) The natural vegetation is made up of [broadleaf forest](/wiki/Temperate_broadleaf_and_mixed_forest "Temperate broadleaf and mixed forest") although conifer plantations are common. Southern Sweden has Sweden's greatest animal and plant diversity.[[16]](#cite_note-NatenSkanebiota-17)[[17]](#cite_note-NatenSkanedjur-18)

The two largest [islands](/wiki/Islands_of_Sweden "Islands of Sweden") are [Gotland](/wiki/Gotland "Gotland") and [Öland](/wiki/%C3%96land "Öland") in the southeast. They differ from the rest of Sweden by being made up of [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") and [marl](/wiki/Marl "Marl") with an [alvar](/wiki/Alvar "Alvar") vegetation adapted to the island's calcareous soils.[[18]](#cite_note-NatenGotlandterrang-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20) Gotland and Öland have [landforms](/wiki/Landform "Landform") that are rare or absent in mainland Sweden. These include [active cliffs](/wiki/Cliff "Cliff") seen in segments of their western coasts,[[20]](#cite_note-Rudberg1967-21) [sea stacks](/wiki/Sea_stack "Sea stack") called *[rauks](/wiki/Rauk "Rauk")* and [large cave systems](/wiki/Solutional_cave "Solutional cave").

## Political divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Political divisions")]

### Provinces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Provinces")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Djur%C3%B6_February_2013_02.jpg/250px-Djur%C3%B6_February_2013_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Djur%C3%B6_February_2013_02.jpg)

[Uppland](/wiki/Uppland "Uppland") province

Main articles: [Provinces of Sweden](/wiki/Provinces_of_Sweden "Provinces of Sweden") and [Lands of Sweden](/wiki/Lands_of_Sweden "Lands of Sweden")

Sweden has 25 [provinces](/wiki/Province "Province") or *landskap* ("landscapes"), based on culture, geography and history: [Bohuslän](/wiki/Bohusl%C3%A4n "Bohuslän"), [Blekinge](/wiki/Blekinge "Blekinge"), [Dalarna](/wiki/Dalarna "Dalarna"), [Dalsland](/wiki/Dalsland "Dalsland"), [Gotland](/wiki/Gotland "Gotland"), [Gästrikland](/wiki/G%C3%A4strikland "Gästrikland"), [Halland](/wiki/Halland "Halland"), [Hälsingland](/wiki/H%C3%A4lsingland "Hälsingland"), [Härjedalen](/wiki/H%C3%A4rjedalen "Härjedalen"), [Jämtland](/wiki/J%C3%A4mtland "Jämtland"), [Lapland](/wiki/Lapland,_Sweden "Lapland, Sweden"), [Medelpad](/wiki/Medelpad "Medelpad"), [Norrbotten](/wiki/Norrbotten "Norrbotten"), [Närke](/wiki/N%C3%A4rke "Närke"), [Skåne](/wiki/Scania "Scania"), [Småland](/wiki/Sm%C3%A5land "Småland"), [Södermanland](/wiki/S%C3%B6dermanland "Södermanland"), [Uppland](/wiki/Uppland "Uppland"), [Värmland](/wiki/V%C3%A4rmland "Värmland"), [Västmanland](/wiki/V%C3%A4stmanland "Västmanland"), [Västerbotten](/wiki/V%C3%A4sterbotten "Västerbotten"), [Västergötland](/wiki/V%C3%A4sterg%C3%B6tland "Västergötland"), [Ångermanland](/wiki/%C3%85ngermanland "Ångermanland"), [Öland](/wiki/%C3%96land "Öland") and [Östergötland](/wiki/%C3%96sterg%C3%B6tland "Östergötland").

While these provinces serve no political or administrative purpose, they play an important role for people's self-identification. The provinces are usually grouped together in three large lands (*landsdelar*): the northern [Norrland](/wiki/Norrland "Norrland"), the central [Svealand](/wiki/Svealand "Svealand") and southern [Götaland](/wiki/G%C3%B6taland "Götaland"). The sparsely populated Norrland encompasses almost 60% of the country.

### Counties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Counties")]

Administratively, Sweden is divided into 21 [counties](/wiki/County "County"), or *län*. In each county there is a [County Administrative Board](/wiki/County_Administrative_Boards_of_Sweden "County Administrative Boards of Sweden"), or *länsstyrelse*, which is appointed by the national government.

In each county there is also a separate [County Council](/wiki/County_Councils_of_Sweden "County Councils of Sweden"), or *region (before 1 januari 2020 called landsting)*, which is the municipal representation appointed by the county electorate.

### Municipalities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Municipalities")]

Main article: [Municipalities of Sweden](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Sweden "Municipalities of Sweden")

[Lapporten](/wiki/Lapporten "Lapporten") mountain pass in [Lapland](/wiki/Lapland,_Sweden "Lapland, Sweden")

Each county is further divided into municipalities or *kommuner*, ranging from only one (in [Gotland County](/wiki/Gotland_County "Gotland County")) to forty-nine (in [Västra Götaland County](/wiki/V%C3%A4stra_G%C3%B6taland_County "Västra Götaland County")). The total number of municipalities is 290.

The northern municipalities are often large in size, but have small populations – the largest municipality is [Kiruna](/wiki/Kiruna "Kiruna") with an area as large as the three southern provinces in Sweden ([Scania](/wiki/Scania "Scania"), [Blekinge](/wiki/Blekinge "Blekinge") and [Halland](/wiki/Halland "Halland")) combined, but it only has a population of 25,000, and its density is about 1 / km2.

## Population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Population")]

Sweden has a population of 10 million as of January 2017.[[21]](#cite_note-22) The mountainous north is considerably less populated than the southern and central regions, partly because the summer period lasts longer in the south, and this is where the more successful agricultural industries were originally established. Another historical reason is said to be the desired proximity to key trade routes and partners in continental Europe, e.g. Germany. As a result, all seven urban areas in Sweden with a population of 100,000 or more, are located in the southern half of the country.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

### Cities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Cities")]

Main articles: [Cities in Sweden](/wiki/Cities_in_Sweden "Cities in Sweden") and [Urban areas in Sweden](/wiki/Urban_areas_in_Sweden "Urban areas in Sweden")

Cities and towns in Sweden are neither political nor administrative entities, but rather localities or [urban areas](/wiki/Urban_areas_in_Sweden "Urban areas in Sweden") independent of municipal subdivisions.

The largest city by population, and the one most significant for culture and media, is the capital of [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm") located in the east, with a population of 1,250,000. The second-largest city is [Gothenburg](/wiki/Gothenburg "Gothenburg"), with a population of 510,500, located in the southwest. The third-largest is [Malmö](/wiki/Malm%C3%B6 "Malmö") in the south, with 258,000. The largest city in the north is [Umeå](/wiki/Ume%C3%A5 "Umeå") with 76,000 inhabitants.

## Natural resources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Natural resources")]

Sweden's [natural resources](/wiki/Natural_resources "Natural resources") include [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper"), [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold"), [hydropower](/wiki/Hydropower "Hydropower"), [iron ore](/wiki/Iron_ore "Iron ore"), [lead](/wiki/Lead "Lead"), [silver](/wiki/Silver "Silver"), [timber](/wiki/Timber "Timber"), [uranium](/wiki/Uranium "Uranium"), and [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc").

## Environment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Environment")]

See also: [Wildlife of Sweden](/wiki/Wildlife_of_Sweden "Wildlife of Sweden") and [Agriculture in Sweden](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Sweden "Agriculture in Sweden")

[Acid rain](/wiki/Acid_rain "Acid rain") has become an issue, as it is damaging [soils](/wiki/Soil "Soil") and [lakes](/wiki/Lake "Lake") and polluting the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") and the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea"). The HBV [hydrology transport model](/wiki/Hydrology_transport_model "Hydrology transport model") has been used to analyze nutrient discharge to the Baltic from tributary watersheds.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Koppen-Geiger_Map_SWE_present.svg/330px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_SWE_present.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_SWE_present.svg)

[Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") types of Sweden

Most of Sweden has a [temperate climate](/wiki/Temperate_climate "Temperate climate"), despite its northern [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"), with largely four distinct seasons and mild temperatures throughout the year. The winter in the far south is usually weak and is manifested only through some shorter periods with snow and sub-zero temperatures, autumn may well turn into spring there, without a distinct period of winter. The northern parts of the country have a [subarctic climate](/wiki/Subarctic_climate "Subarctic climate") while the central parts have a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate"). The coastal south can be defined as having either a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") using the 0 °C isotherm, or an [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") using the –3 °C isotherm.

Due to the increased maritime moderation in the peninsular south, temperature differences between the coastlines of the southernmost and northernmost regions are about 2 °C (4 °F) in summer and 10 °C (18 °F) in winter. This grows further when comparing areas in the northern interior where the winter difference in the far north is about 15 °C (27 °F) throughout the country. The warmest summers usually happen in the [Mälaren Valley](/wiki/M%C3%A4laren_Valley "Mälaren Valley") around [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm")[[23]](#cite_note-24) due to the vast landmass shielding the middle east coast from Atlantic low-pressure systems in July compared to the south and west. Daytime highs in Sweden's municipal seats vary from 19 °C (66 °F) to 24 °C (75 °F) in July and −9 °C (16 °F) to 3 °C (37 °F) in January. The colder temperatures are influenced by the higher elevation in the northern interior. At sea level instead, the coldest average highs range from 21 °C (70 °F) to −6 °C (21 °F). As a result of the mild summers, the arctic region of [Norrbotten](/wiki/Norrbotten_County "Norrbotten County") has some of the northernmost [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") in the world.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

Sweden is much warmer and drier than other places at a similar latitude, and even somewhat farther south, mainly because of the combination of the [Gulf Stream](/wiki/Gulf_Stream "Gulf Stream")[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27) and the general west wind drift, caused by the direction of planet Earth's rotation. Continental west-coasts (to which all of Scandinavia belongs, as the westernmost part of the [Eurasian continent](/wiki/Eurasia "Eurasia")), are notably warmer than continental east-coasts; this can also be seen by comparing e.g. the Canadian cities of [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver") and [Halifax, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Halifax, Nova Scotia") with each other, the winter in west coast Vancouver is much milder; also, for example, central and southern Sweden has much milder winters than many parts of Russia, Canada, and the northern United States.[[27]](#cite_note-28) Because of Sweden's high latitude, the length of daylight varies greatly. North of the Arctic Circle, the [sun never sets](/wiki/Midnight_sun "Midnight sun") for part of each summer, and [it never rises](/wiki/Polar_night "Polar night") for part of each winter. In the capital, [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), daylight lasts for more than 18 hours in late June but only around 6 hours in late December. Sweden receives between 1,100 and 1,900 hours of sunshine annually.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

The highest temperature ever recorded in Sweden was 38 °C (100 °F) in [Målilla](/wiki/M%C3%A5lilla "Målilla") in June 1947,[[29]](#cite_note-SMHI-30) a record shared with [Ultuna](/wiki/Ultuna "Ultuna") in [Uppland](/wiki/Uppland "Uppland").[[29]](#cite_note-SMHI-30) The coldest temperature ever recorded was −52.6 °C (−62.7 °F) in Vuoggatjålme on 2 February 1966.[[30]](#cite_note-31) Temperatures expected in Sweden are heavily influenced by the large Fennoscandian landmass, as well as continental Europe and western Russia, which allows hot or cool inland air to be easily transported to Sweden. That, in turn, renders most of Sweden's southern areas having warmer summers than almost everywhere in the nearby [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"), even matching temperatures found along the continental Atlantic coast as far south as in northern Spain. In winter, however, the same high-pressure systems sometimes put the entire country far below freezing temperatures. There is some maritime moderation from the Atlantic which renders the Swedish continental climate less severe than that of nearby Russia. Even though temperature patterns differ between north and south, the summer climate is surprisingly similar all through the entire country in spite of the large latitudinal differences. This is due to the south's being surrounded by a greater mass of water, with the wider Baltic Sea and the Atlantic air passing over lowland areas from the south-west.

Apart from the ice-free Atlantic bringing marine air into Sweden tempering winters, the mildness is further explained by prevailing low-pressure systems postponing winter, with the long nights often staying above freezing in the south of the country due to the abundant cloud cover. By the time winter finally breaks through, daylight hours rise quickly, ensuring that daytime temperatures soar quickly in spring. With the greater number of clear nights, frosts remain commonplace quite far south as late as April. The cold winters occur when low-pressure systems are weaker. An example is that the coldest ever month (January 1987) in Stockholm was also the sunniest January month on record.[[31]](#cite_note-32)[[32]](#cite_note-33)

The relative strength of low and high-pressure systems of marine and continental air also define the highly variable summers. When hot continental air hits the country, the long days and short nights frequently bring temperatures up to 30 °C (86 °F) or above even in coastal areas. Nights normally remain cool, especially in inland areas. Coastal areas can see so-called *tropical nights* above 20 °C (68 °F) occur due to the moderating sea influence during warmer summers.[[33]](#cite_note-34) Summers can be cool, especially in the north of the country. Transitional seasons are normally quite extensive and the four-season climate applies to most of Sweden's territory, except in Scania where some years do not record a [meteorological winter](/wiki/Meteorological_winter "Meteorological winter") (see table below) or in the high Lapland mountains where polar microclimates exist.

On average, most of Sweden receives between 500 and 800 mm (20 and 31 in) of precipitation each year, making it considerably drier than the [global average](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)"). The south-western part of the country receives more precipitation, between 1,000 and 1,200 mm (39 and 47 in), and some mountain areas in the north are estimated to receive up to 2,000 mm (79 in). Despite northerly locations, southern and central Sweden may have almost no snow in some winters. Most of Sweden is located in the [rain shadow](/wiki/Rain_shadow "Rain shadow") of the Scandinavian Mountains through Norway and north-west Sweden. The blocking of cool and wet air in summer, as well as the greater landmass, leads to warm and dry summers far north in the country, with quite warm summers at the Bothnia Bay coast at 65 degrees latitude, which is unheard of elsewhere in the world at such northerly coastlines.

It is predicted that as the [Barents Sea](/wiki/Barents_Sea "Barents Sea") gets less frozen in the coming winters, becoming thus "Atlantified", additional evaporation will increase future snowfalls in Sweden and much of continental Europe.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

Extreme points of Sweden

The extreme points of Sweden include the coordinates that are farthest north, south, east and west in Sweden, and the ones that are at the highest and the lowest elevations in the country. Unlike Norway and Denmark, Sweden has no external territories that can be considered either inside or outside the country depending on definition, meaning that the extreme points of Sweden are unambiguous.

The latitude and longitude are expressed in [decimal degree notation](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system#Degrees:_a_measurement_of_angle "Geographic coordinate system"), in which a positive latitude value refers to the Northern Hemisphere, and a negative value refers to the Southern Hemisphere. Additionally, a negative elevation value refers to land below sea level. The coordinates used in this article are sourced from [Google Earth](/wiki/Google_Earth "Google Earth"), which makes use of the [World Geodetic System](/wiki/World_Geodetic_System "World Geodetic System") (WGS) 84, a geodetic reference system.

### Latitude and longitude

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Latitude and longitude")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Treriks.jpg/250px-Treriks.jpg)](/wiki/File:Treriks.jpg)

[Treriksröset](/wiki/Treriksr%C3%B6set "Treriksröset"), Sweden's northernmost point

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Smygehuk-1.jpg/250px-Smygehuk-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Smygehuk-1.jpg)

Signpost in the harbour of [Smygehuk](/wiki/Smygehuk "Smygehuk"), Sweden's southernmost point

Sweden's northernmost point is [Treriksröset](/wiki/Treriksr%C3%B6set "Treriksröset"), in the [Lapland](/wiki/Lapland,_Sweden "Lapland, Sweden") province,[[40]](#cite_note-cia-41) where the borders of Sweden, Norway, and Finland meet. The closest Swedish city to the area is [Kiruna](/wiki/Kiruna "Kiruna"), which is Sweden's northernmost city.[[41]](#cite_note-google_trerik-42) Sweden's southernmost point is in the harbour of the fishing village Smygehuk, near the city of [Trelleborg](/wiki/Trelleborg "Trelleborg"),[[42]](#cite_note-trelleborg-43) which borders the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea").[[43]](#cite_note-google_smyge-44) At the [pier](/wiki/Pier "Pier") of the harbour, a signpost displays the exact position of the point, as well as the distance to Treriksröset, [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), and [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow").[[42]](#cite_note-trelleborg-43)

Sweden's westernmost point is on Stora Drammen, an [islet](/wiki/Islet "Islet") in [Skagerrak](/wiki/Skagerrak "Skagerrak") outside the coast of [Bohuslän](/wiki/Bohusl%C3%A4n "Bohuslän").[[44]](#cite_note-scb-45) [Seabirds](/wiki/Seabirds "Seabirds") and [harbor seals](/wiki/Harbor_seal "Harbor seal") have colonies on the islet, but it is uninhabited by humans.[[45]](#cite_note-46) Sweden's easternmost point is on [Kataja](/wiki/Kataja "Kataja"),[[44]](#cite_note-scb-45) an islet south of [Haparanda](/wiki/Haparanda "Haparanda") in the [Bothnian Bay](/wiki/Bothnian_Bay "Bothnian Bay").[[46]](#cite_note-atlas-47) The islet is divided between Sweden and Finland. The border was established in 1809, after the [Finnish War](/wiki/Finnish_War "Finnish War"), between what was previously two islets, a Swedish one called Kataja and a smaller Finnish one called Inakari. Since 1809, [post-glacial rebound](/wiki/Post-glacial_rebound "Post-glacial rebound") has caused the sea level in the region to drop relative to land level, joining the two islets.[[47]](#cite_note-48) If counting the [mainland](/wiki/Mainland "Mainland") only, Stensvik in [Strömstad](/wiki/Str%C3%B6mstad "Strömstad") is Sweden's westernmost point,[[48]](#cite_note-eniro_stensvik-49) and Sundholmen in [Haparanda](/wiki/Haparanda "Haparanda") is the easternmost point.[[49]](#cite_note-sundholm-50)

### Elevation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Elevation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Kebnekaise_view_from_Tuolpagorni.jpg/250px-Kebnekaise_view_from_Tuolpagorni.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kebnekaise_view_from_Tuolpagorni.jpg)

At 2,097 metres (6,880 ft), [Kebnekaise](/wiki/Kebnekaise "Kebnekaise") is Sweden's highest point.

The highest point in Sweden is the northern peak of Kebnekaise, which stands at 2,097 metres (6,880 ft). It is in the [Scandinavian Mountains](/wiki/Scandinavian_Mountains "Scandinavian Mountains") chain, in the province of Lapland.[[61]](#cite_note-dn-62)[[62]](#cite_note-google_kebnekaise-63) The mountain has two peaks, of which the [glaciated](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier") southern one was until fairly recently the highest at above 2,100 metres (6,890 ft). The top glacier on the southern peak has shrunk fast; therefore the summit is not as high as earlier. It was 2,104 metres (6,903 ft) in 2008.[[61]](#cite_note-dn-62)
The northern peak, which stands at 2,097 metres (6,880 ft), is free of ice. Other points of comparable height in the vicinity of Kebnekaise include Sarektjåkka at 2,089 metres (6,854 ft), and Kaskasatjåkka at 2,076 metres (6,811 ft).[[44]](#cite_note-scb-45)

Sweden's lowest point, which is 2.41 metres (7.91 ft) below sea level, is in the [Kristianstads Vattenrike Biosphere Reserve](/wiki/Kristianstads_Vattenrike_Biosphere_Reserve "Kristianstads Vattenrike Biosphere Reserve") in the city of [Kristianstad](/wiki/Kristianstad "Kristianstad").[[40]](#cite_note-cia-41) The point is at the bottom of what was once Nosabyviken, a bay on the lake of Hammarsjön. The bay was drained in the 1860s by John Nun Milner, an engineer, to get more [arable land](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land") for Kristianstad.[[63]](#cite_note-vattenriket-64)

### Historically

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Historically")]

Northernmost:

* before 1751: unclear (undefined border)
* 1751–1809: [Nuorgam](/wiki/Nuorgam "Nuorgam"), Finland

Southernmost:

* Before 1648: [south cape of Öland](/wiki/L%C3%A5nge_Jan "Långe Jan")
* 1648-1815: [Swedish Pomerania](/wiki/Swedish_Pomerania "Swedish Pomerania")

Westernmost:

* Before 1658: [Vinga](/wiki/Vinga_(Gothenburg) "Vinga (Gothenburg)")
* 1638–1655: as a colony, [New Sweden](/wiki/New_Sweden "New Sweden")

Easternmost:

* 1617–1721: [Ingria](/wiki/Ingria "Ingria")
